heading: CHE GOVERNA E MODERA TUTTE LE COSE
card_summary: This chunk opens the argument that Christ is the true Lord who governs and moderates all things, marshalling a dense chain of prophetic testimonies from Moses, David, Isaiah, Jeremiah, and other biblical sources, all interpreted through a Christological and Kabbalistic lens. Zorzi begins with the double account of creation in Genesis, arguing on kabbalistic grounds (from the "sacrosanct receptacles," i.e., the Sefirot or kabbalistic commentaries) that Moses first described the creation of the Messiah and his mother. He then works through Jacob's blessing of Judah (Genesis 49:10), citing the Targum Onkelos and David Qimchi to identify "Shiloh" as the Messiah, and refuting multiple Jewish counter-readings (Nebuchadnezzar, Saul, Jeroboam, Ahijah of Shiloh). The chunk also engages Isaiah's "El gibbor" (Mighty God), Jeremiah's "Branch of David" with the Targum of Yonatan ben Uzziel, Psalm 110 ("Dixit Dominus"), and treats the Tetragrammaton's four letters hidden in the name "Judah" as a Kabbalistic cipher for the Incarnation.
essay:
Now I'll expand this essay from approximately 1,200 words to 2,000-4,000 words by adding 2-3 substantive scholarly paragraphs per section while preserving all existing blockquotes, citations, and formatting.

## Overview

Chunk 81 belongs to the *Secondo Cantico*, Tono Settimo, of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* and constitutes one of his most sustained exercises in prophetic Christology read through Kabbalistic philology. The presiding claim — announced in the section title — is that Christ is the true ruler who governs and moderates all things. Zorzi builds this case exegetically, marching through Mosaic, Davidic, Isaian, and Jeremiac testimony, at each step deploying Aramaic targumim and Hebrew philology to foreclose non-Christological Jewish readings.

The larger philosophical context situating this chunk within *De Harmonia Mundi* involves the fundamental problem of cosmic order: if God is absolutely transcendent and infinitely removed from materiality, how does divine order penetrate and structure the visible universe? Zorzi's answer, inherited from Neoplatonic and Kabbalistic traditions, positions the Logos — the Word, identified with Christ — as the principle of mediation between the eternal intelligible realm and the temporal, sensible world. This chunk demonstrates that this mediation is not merely speculative or allegorical but grounded in the literal testimony of Scripture, interpreted through the Kabbalistic notion that the Hebrew language itself encodes divine mysteries. By reading the Old Testament through targumim, midrashic traditions, and the Sefirot-based commentary, Zorzi constructs a *philological proof* that the Messiah was always the true governor of all things, both before and after the Incarnation.

---

## The Double Creation Narrative and the Kabbalistic Priority of the Messiah

Zorzi opens with a problem in Genesis: Moses appears to narrate the creation of humanity twice. A man of Moses's stature — "eruditissimo in ogni forma di sapienza, ricolmo di Dio e perfuso dallo Spirito santo" — could not simply have forgotten what he wrote. The solution, drawn from "sacrosanct receptacles" (*sacrosancti receptacula*), the standard Zorzian term for kabbalistic sources (likely referring to the Zohar or to the Sefirot-based commentary tradition), is that the first creation account describes the Messiah and his mother:

> Di quei due Cristo è la vera immagine di Dio e il primogenito di tutte le cose, come dice Paolo: Egli è l'immagine del Dio invisibile, il primogenito di ogni creatura. Egli fu fatto il maschio in sé e la femmina in sé, i primi nella mente divina, prima e al di sopra di tutti gli altri.

<cite>p. 1642 (Italian side)</cite>

The "male and female" of Genesis 1:27 are thus the Messiah and the Church (or his mother), not Adam and Eve — the "maschio e femmina" to whom the command "Crescete e moltiplicatevi" was addressed, understood spiritually, not carnally. The reference is to John 1:16 ("dalla pienezza... tutti abbiamo ricevuto"). Paul's Colossians 1:15 is the hinge text.

This exegetical strategy is rooted in a particular reading of Kabbalistic cosmology, specifically the doctrine of the Sefirot or divine emanations. In the Kabbalistic tradition, the Sefirot represent both the structure of the divine mind and the structure of creation itself: Chokmah (Wisdom) and Binah (Understanding) are often read as primordial male and female principles that generate all subsequent emanations and all created beings. Zorzi's identification of the first creation account with Christ and the Church reflects the Christological interpretation of these principles that was developing in late medieval and early modern Kabbalah-Christian circles. The Kabbalists themselves understood Chokmah as the transcendent thought of God, and Binah as the receptive matrix through which that thought unfolds. By equating Christ with primordial Wisdom and the Church with Binah-as-receptivity, Zorzi positions the Incarnation not as a break in cosmic history but as the revelation of an eternal archetypal structure already present in creation's foundation.

The Pauline "image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15-16) was the standard proof-text in Christological theology for Christ's role as the principle through which all things were made. But Zorzi's innovation is to read this backwards: if Christ is the true image of God according to Paul, and if the first creation account depicts the "image of God" (Genesis 1:27), then the first creation account necessarily depicts Christ. This is a form of what medieval exegetes called *typology* — reading the Old Testament as a coded prefiguration of New Testament reality — but grounded not merely in narrative analogy but in Kabbalistic linguistic philosophy. The Hebrew letters and numerical values themselves are understood as encoding eternal truths. The first creation is thus "prior" to the second not just narratively but ontologically: it represents the intelligible creation, the eternal archetypes in the divine mind, while the second creation account (Genesis 2) represents the sensible creation in time and space, the fallen material world that requires redemption.

---

## The Blessing of Judah (Genesis 49:10) and the Targum Onkelos

The next major movement concerns Jacob's deathbed blessing: "Non sarà tolto lo scettro da Giuda... finché venga Shiloh." Zorzi cites Targum Onkelos's Aramaic rendering of "Shiloh" as *mashicha* (the Messiah), and quotes the Targum at length in Hebrew/Aramaic characters (rendered imperfectly in the digital transcription due to encoding):

> Non cesserà il sultano dalla casata di Giuda né lo scriba, o il dottore dai figli dei suoi figli fino al secolo in cui verrà il Messia: suo sarà il regno e a lui obbediranno i popoli, o le genti.

<cite>p. 1647 (Italian side, paraphrase of Targum Onkelos on Gen. 49:10)</cite>

Zorzi endorses David Qimchi's (*Radak*'s) reading and systematically dismantles four Jewish counter-interpretations:

1. **Nebuchadnezzar as Shiloh**: rejected on philological grounds ("Shiloh" does not mean "sent") and historical grounds (the scepter of Judah was not fully removed under him; Zerubbabel and subsequent leaders continued the line).
2. **Saul as fulfilment**: rejected because the scepter was only briefly loaned to Benjamin and immediately restored to Judah in David; moreover, Saul was not anointed at Shiloh but at Kiriath-Jearim or in the land of Zuph (1 Samuel).
3. **Jeroboam as fulfilment**: rejected because the kingdom was merely diminished, not removed from Judah, which continued through Rehoboam for twelve more generations.
4. **Ahijah of Shiloh as the referent**: rejected because this reading cannot sustain the continuity of the scepter claim.

The philological kernel of Zorzi's argument involves the Hebrew word *shevet* (שבט), which he notes means both "staff of a leader" and "royal scepter," citing the Song of Deborah (Judges 5) regarding Zebulun — "Da Zabulon, colui che trascina, o comanda, con il bastone dello scriba" — to show that *shevet* continued to apply to non-royal leaders after the Babylonian captivity. This permits the claim that the scepter in the broad sense persisted until Herod the Idumean, in whose thirty-first regnal year Christ was born.

The targumim, or Aramaic paraphrases of Scripture, occupied a peculiar and crucial position in Jewish and Christian-Jewish hermeneutics of the medieval and early modern periods. Unlike the Hebrew Bible, which Jewish interpretation often treated as deliberately ambiguous so as to admit multiple legitimate readings, the targumim represented an older authoritative tradition of interpretation, often attributed to tannaitic or amoraic sages of the first few centuries CE. For Christian interpreters like Zorzi, the targumim were invaluable precisely because they seemed to represent Jewish tradition *before* the Christian interpretation was possible, yet they frequently contained apparent Messianic readings that aligned with Christian doctrine. The Targum Onkelos's rendering of "Shiloh" as *mashicha* is a famous example: the Hebrew word Shiloh (שִׁילֹה) is of uncertain etymology and meaning, and the Targum's interpretive gloss in Aramaic (*meshicha*, "Messiah") was read by Christian exegetes as evidence that ancient Jewish tradition itself understood this verse Messianically — even if later Jewish interpreters, faced with Christian claims, had backed away from that interpretation.

Zorzi's refutation of four competing Jewish interpretations is methodologically sophisticated: he does not argue merely that Christ is a better fit to the prophecy than these alternatives, but rather that none of these alternatives actually satisfy the conditions laid down in the text itself. If Nebuchadnezzar is the referent, then the scepter must have been "removed from Judah" under him; but history shows it was not — Zerubbabel and his descendants continued to lead in the post-exilic period. If Saul is the referent, the scepter cannot have been removed from Judah itself, since Saul reigned over Israel from Benjamin; moreover, the text explicitly associates Shiloh with blessing and peaceful reign, while Saul's reign was marked by internal strife and jealousy toward David. This kind of argument — working through counter-interpretations with scriptural and historical precision — reflects the scholastic method that Zorzi inherited and adapts. The point is not to dismiss Jewish interpretation as a whole, but to show that a rigorous, internally consistent reading of the text, combined with historical fact, leaves the Christological interpretation as the only tenable solution.

---

## The Tetragrammaton Hidden in the Name "Judah"

One of the chunk's most characteristically Kabbalistic moments is Zorzi's phonological analysis of the Hebrew name יהודה (*Yehudah*, "Judah"):

> (cid:676)h(cid:722)(cid:676)(cid:653)(cid:681), Giuda, è scritto in ebraico con le quattro lettere del grande nome Tetragramma con l'inserzione di (cid:675), dalet, che equivale al numero quattro, cioè la struttura quaternaria della massa corporea.

<cite>p. 1648 (Italian side)</cite>

The name יהודה contains the four letters of the divine name יהוה (YHWH) with the insertion of *dalet* (ד), whose numerical value (4) symbolizes the "quaternary structure of corporeal mass" — i.e., the fourfold constitution of the material body. This is the Kabbalistic argument for the Incarnation: the name Judah enciphers the Word made flesh, the divine Name entering the quaternary physical world. The Psalmist's cry "Judah is my king" (*Iehuda rex meus*, Psalm 60/108) thus secretly proclaims "the Tetragrammaton incarnate is my king."

This passage exemplifies the Kabbalistic practice of *gematria* — the assignment of numerical values to Hebrew letters and the discovery of hidden meanings through mathematical correspondences. In classical Kabbalah, gematria was understood not as a mechanical game but as a window into the deep structure of reality: since Hebrew was believed to be the language of creation (the language through which God spoke the world into being), the numerical patterns embedded in Hebrew words were thought to reflect metaphysical truths. The number four (*arba'ah* in Hebrew) held profound significance in Kabbalistic cosmology: it represented the four worlds of emanation (Atziluth, Briah, Yetzirah, Assiyyah — the world of Emanation, Creation, Formation, and Action); it also represented the four-fold nature of the material body in scholastic philosophy (composed of four elements: fire, air, water, earth, or understood as matter, form, and the four complexions).

Zorzi's argument works on multiple levels simultaneously. First, it is a philological observation: *yod-hei-vav-dalet-hei* (יְהוּדָה, the full spelling of Judah in Hebrew) does indeed contain the four letters of the Tetragrammaton *yod-hei-vav-hei* (יְהוָה) with a *dalet* inserted. Second, it is a Kabbalistic interpretation: the insertion of *dalet* (whose numerical value is 4) transforms the sacred Name into a word that can be spoken, that can designate a specific people, that can incarnate in history. The divine name, unpronounceable and transcendent, is "corporalized" through the addition of the number 4, the mark of materiality. Thus, the very name of the tribe of Judah encodes the Incarnation: it says that the Tetragrammaton — God Himself — has become flesh in a particular people, at a particular time, with a particular name.

The theological implication is profound: this is not an accident or a late addition to Hebrew nomenclature but a feature present in the foundational linguistic material of Scripture. The Incarnation is not a surprise interruption of history but is encoded in the very language through which God revealed Himself to Israel. If the name Judah contains the divine Name plus the sign of material reality, then the Messiah-Christ who comes from Judah does not represent a violation of the divine transcendence but its ultimate fulfillment: God becomes human not by abandoning His divinity but by expressing it through the created order that He sustains. This is consistent with Neoplatonic metaphysics, where the One (or God) does not emanate or descend into matter but rather matter participates in the intelligible forms that eternally proceed from the divine source.

---

## The Extension of Christ's Kingdom to the Gentiles: Jacob's Blessing Continued

Zorzi proceeds through the rest of Genesis 49:11–12, reading allegorically following Jerome:

- The **foal** (*pullus*) bound to the vine = the Gentile peoples, new in faith, son of mercy and grace, bound to Christ who said "I am the vine."
- The **she-ass** (*asina*) = the mother Church, whose children are both Gentile and Israelite peoples (like the parable of the two sons, Luke 15).
- The **washing of the garment in wine / blood of the grape** = the Eucharist, and/or the Gentile people covering the shame of Christ's passion, purified by his blood.

> Questo è il calice del mio sangue, della nuova ed eterna alleanza, versato per voi e per molti in remissione dei peccati.

<cite>p. 1650 (Italian side, quoting the words of institution)</cite>

This Eucharistic reading provides the basis for Christ's legitimate dominion: he acquired lordship over the human race, the angelic nature, and all things through his Passion and death. Paul's Philippians 2:8–10 ("obedient unto death... every knee shall bow") is the doctrinal summation.

Zorzi's allegorical reading of the Judah blessing is rooted in patristic and medieval exegetical tradition, particularly in Jerome's commentaries, which sought to extract typological and spiritual meanings from the patriarchal narratives. But Zorzi's application is distinctly his own: he reads the imagery of the vine and the wine not merely as a symbol of spiritual blessing but as a specific reference to the Eucharist and to the incorporation of the Gentiles into the mystical body of Christ. The "foal bound to the vine" becomes a figure for the Gentile Church, which has no natural claim to covenant blessing (the vine is Israel's traditional symbol) but is incorporated through grace and faith. The "she-ass" — a lowly beast of burden — represents the humility of the Church, servant of all, bearing the weight of redemptive history.

The Eucharistic dimension is crucial to understanding Zorzi's argument about governance and moderation. In sacramental theology, the Eucharist is understood as the perpetual mystical reenactment of Christ's sacrifice, the means by which Christ's saving death is made present to each generation of believers. If Christ governs the world through the Eucharist, then His rule is not merely external (through political force or natural law) but intimate and interior (through the transformation of believers into His mystical body). The wine-washing of the garment refers not only to the historical Passion but to the ongoing mystical participation of the Church in Christ's redemptive work. This connects directly to the *harmonia mundi* theme: cosmic order is maintained not by abstract principles or mechanical laws but by the perpetual self-offering of the Incarnate Word, mystically renewed in the sacrament. The Gentile peoples, once "strangers to the covenant," become heirs through this sacramental incorporation, their violent and chaotic histories transformed into acts of participation in redemptive history.

---

## The Prophetic Dossier: David, Isaiah, Jeremiah

In the second subsection (Chapter II), Zorzi amasses additional prophetic witnesses:

**Psalm 2** ("Tu sei mio figlio, oggi ti ho generato"): the Hebrew *ha-yom* ("today") is glossed as meaning *eternity* without temporal determination, citing the author of a work called *Discorsi di bellezza* (*Eloquia pulchritudinis*, possibly a reference to a kabbalistic or rabbinic text). The verse thus simultaneously affirms eternal generation and temporal birth.

**The "Islamic schism"** is treated parenthetically: the Muhammadan apostasy is cited as the fulfilment of Paul's prophecy in 2 Thessalonians 2 about the "man of sin" who precedes the Parousia. Zorzi asserts that Christ will destroy this Antichrist "with the breath of his mouth" (2 Thess. 2:8) and then rule with an iron scepter (Psalm 2:9).

**Isaiah 9:6**: "El gibbor, Padre del mondo a venire" — Zorzi insists this cannot mean "strong strong" (which would be redundant) and must apply solely to the Messiah who is simultaneously God and man. Jewish readings applying this to Hezekiah are dismissed on historical grounds: the Davidic royal line is long extinct, as attested by Josephus and Eusebius.

**Jeremiah 23:5–6** ("germoglio giusto"): Zorzi quotes the Targum of Yonatan ben Uzziel (*Jonathan ben Uzziel*) in Aramaic, which renders "righteous branch" as *meshicha de-tzidka* ("the just Messiah"):

> E susciterò per Davide il Messia giusto, cioè susciterò nella stirpe o nella casa di Davide il Messia giusto. Egli regnerà da vero re e sarà saggio, ed eserciterà il diritto e la giustizia sulla terra.

<cite>p. 1655 (Italian side, quoting Targum Jonathan on Jer. 23:5)</cite>

The Jeremiac name *Adonay tzidqenu* ("the Lord our righteousness"), which Zorzi notes cannot lawfully be applied to any mere human since no man may bear the divine Tetragrammaton — citing Maimonides (*Mosè l'egiziano*, the *Guide for the Perplexed*) — clinches the Christological reading.

**Psalm 110** ("Dixit Dominus Domino meo"): Zorzi invokes Christ's own self-exegesis in the Gospels — if David calls the Messiah his Lord, who among his subjects or foreigners could he mean? Only the Messiah already seen in spirit as Lord of all, as Paul explains.

The accumulation of these prophetic witnesses is strategically designed to create what Renaissance theologians called a *dossier* — a body of incontrovertible testimonies that, taken together, constitute a proof. Each prophecy individually might be contested; together, they form an overdetermined convergence on the figure of the Messiah as the eternal Lord and King. Zorzi's method reflects the practice of proof-texting common in medieval and early modern theology, but with a specific Kabbalistic inflection: the prophecies are not merely predictions that came to pass but revelations encoded in the very structure of Hebrew language and history.

The gloss on Psalm 2:7 — understanding *ha-yom* as denoting eternity rather than temporal immediacy — requires special attention. This is not Zorzi's innovation; it is drawn from rabbinic and medieval Jewish commentary, which grappled with the apparent contradiction between "today I have begotten you" (suggesting a temporal generation) and Christian affirmations of Christ's eternal, non-temporal nature. The solution — to read *ha-yom* as a mystical "today" that transcends time — was developed in Jewish circles as a way of preserving the verse's relevance to Messianic doctrine while avoiding the implication that God begat the Messiah in time. Zorzi adopts and Christianizes this reading, turning it into evidence that Scripture itself testifies to the both/and nature of Christ's generation: eternal in the divine mind, temporal in history.

Isaiah 9:6's titles for the coming king — "Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Eternal Father, Prince of Peace" (*Pele-Yo'etz, El-Gibbor, Avi-'Ad, Sar-Shalom*) — were perennially disputed between Jewish and Christian interpreters. The Christian reading, which Zorzi endorses, takes "El gibbor" (Mighty God) as a clear statement of the Messiah's divinity. The Jewish objection was that the phrase means "mighty power" or "strong might" — a poetic redundancy expressing divine strength without necessarily identifying a person as God Himself. Zorzi's counter is sharp: if the prophet means to say merely "mighty power," why use the grammatical form that designates a singular being? The phrase is structured to describe a person (the Messiah-king of verse 6a) by means of titles that attribute to that person the epithets of God. Applied to Hezekiah (as some Jewish interpreters suggested), the titles become absurd: Hezekiah was a righteous king but not "Mighty God" or "Eternal Father" in any literal sense. Applied to Christ, who is understood in Christian doctrine as both fully human and fully divine, the titles achieve their literal meaning.

The name *Adonay tzidqenu* — "the Lord our righteousness" — given to the messianic branch in Jeremiah 23:6 is perhaps the most directly Christological title in the Hebrew Bible from the perspective of Christian interpreters. Zorzi's appeal to Maimonides is here particularly clever: Maimonides, in the *Guide for the Perplexed* III.41, explicitly discusses the prohibition against attributing the divine name to any human being and uses this as a basis for rejecting Christian Incarnational claims. By citing Maimonides's own principle, Zorzi shows that even the greatest Jewish philosopher acknowledges that a mere human cannot be called by the Tetragrammaton or its synonyms. Therefore, if Jeremiah applies such a name to the messianic branch, that branch cannot be a mere human — it must be the divine Logos incarnate. This is sophisticated argument using Jewish philosophy against Jewish anti-Christian interpretation.

---

## Technical Terms and Kabbalistic Apparatus

- **Sacrosancti receptacula** (sacrosanct receptacles): Zorzi's standard Latin for kabbalistic depositories of esoteric knowledge, likely referencing Zoharic or midrashic material.
- **Shiloh / שִׁילֹה** and *mashicha* (משיחא): the Aramaic Messianic rendering in Targum Onkelos.
- **Shevet / שֵׁבֶט**: staff/scepter — Zorzi's Hebrew philological argument.
- **We-lo yiqqehat 'ammim / וְלוֹ יִקְּהַת עַמִּים**: "to him shall be the obedience of the peoples" — cited as the literal Hebrew of Gen. 49:10d.
- **El gibbor / אֵל גִּבּוֹר**: "Mighty God" (Isaiah 9:5) — used to establish Messianic divinity.
- **Adonay tzidqenu / יְהוָה צִדְקֵנוּ**: "the LORD our righteousness" (Jeremiah 23:6) — used to identify the Messiah with the divine Tetragrammaton.
- **Ha-yom / הַיּוֹם**: "today" (Psalm 2:7) — glossed as denoting eternity, via a source called *Eloquia pulchritudinis*.
- **Dalet / ד**: fourth letter, numerical value 4, representing the quaternary structure of the body; inserted into YHWH to form the name Judah.

---

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk is a key node in Zorzi's macro-argument that the cosmic harmony (*harmonia mundi*) is governed by the Messiah-Logos who was present in the divine mind before creation. The double creation narrative, interpreted Kabbalistically, grounds Christ's lordship in the primordial order itself. The philological argument about the Tetragrammaton in the name Judah is a microcosm of Zorzi's broader thesis that Hebrew Scripture, properly read through Kabbalistic philology, encodes the full Christological mystery. The chunk also integrates Zorzi's eschatological concerns — the Islamic schism, the coming Antichrist, the iron scepter — into his harmony cosmology, suggesting that temporal disorder is itself part of a providential structure culminating in Christ's universal and perpetual reign.

The entire argument of this chunk exemplifies what might be called Zorzi's *harmonic hermeneutic*: the conviction that all of Scripture, properly understood, testifies to a unified cosmic principle of order centered on the Messiah-Logos. The multiple prophecies are not separate texts making independent claims but rather variations on a single eternal theme, much as a musical theme might be expressed through different instruments and registers. The Mosaic narratives, the Davidic psalms, the prophetic books of Isaiah and Jeremiah — these are all expressions, in the historical and linguistic register, of a single metaphysical reality: that the Logos who governs all things is the same Christ whose Incarnation is the hinge of history. The Kabbalistic apparatus (the Sefirot, gematria, the encoding of divine secrets in Hebrew letters) is not ornamental exegetical play but the method through which this underlying unity becomes visible to those with eyes to see. The "sacrosanct receptacles" of Kabbalistic tradition preserve, in their own language, the same truths that Christian theology articulates through the frameworks of Neoplatonic metaphysics and trinitarian dogma. By bringing these traditions into dialogue — reading the Hebrew Bible through Aramaic targumim and Kabbalistic philology, interpreting both through Christian doctrinal frameworks — Zorzi constructs an integrated philosophical and theological vision in which creation, revelation, incarnation, and redemption all flow from a single source and aim toward a single end: the manifestation of the divine harmony throughout the cosmos.
